meta.title: Citan Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: Mercedes • Citan • 2012–present • W415 • W420 • OM607 • OM608 • K9K
hero.h1: Citan Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every Mercedes Citan generation — from the original W415 (2012–2021) to the second-generation W420 (2021–present). Full coverage of Renault-sourced 1.5 dCi diesel reliability, 1.3 petrol options, common injector and turbo failures, and real UK replacement economics for the UK's most practical Mercedes van. Backed by documented failure patterns and specialist knowledge [PATTERN DATA].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The Citan rule: commercial maths — a £1,200–£2,800 reconditioned engine against a £3,000–£12,000 van; the economics depend entirely on whether the van earns its keep. For a working van, replacement often makes sense; for a private MPV, check the numbers carefully.
OM608 1.5 dCi (Euro 6, 2018+) — the revised Renault-sourced diesel with improved injector design and AdBlue; fewer failure reports than the earlier OM607.
OM607 1.5 dCi (Euro 5, 2012–2018) — injector failure and turbo issues are the defining weaknesses; cold-start problems and loss of power are the early warnings.
Citan W415 110 CDI (2018–2021) with OM608 — the revised Euro 6 diesel with AdBlue, improved injector design, and the facelift interior; the sweet spot of reliability and value.
OM607 injector failure — the defining weakness of the early 1.5 dCi; injector replacement costs €515+ (£440+).

🔧 Commercial Workhorse — the Citan is Mercedes' entry-level van, sharing its bones with the Renault Kangoo. Buy it for the three-pointed star on a practical, affordable package — but understand you're paying a premium for the badge.

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The Citan is a Renault Kangoo with a Mercedes badge — and that's not a bad thing. The 1.5 dCi diesel is proven and economical, but the early OM607 engines have injector and turbo weaknesses that demand careful inspection. Buy a 2018+ facelift with the revised OM608 diesel and full service history, and it'll earn its keep.

OM607 1.5 dCi (Euro 5, 2012–2018) — injector and turbo failures
H2: Mercedes Citan Generations — Find Your Vehicle

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Two generations of Mercedes' smallest commercial vehicle — the original W415 (2012–2021) and the second-generation W420 (2021–present), both sharing Renault-sourced engines and a practical, van-based layout.

Our Verdict: The original Citan — a rebadged Renault Kangoo with a Mercedes grille. The 1.5 dCi diesel is the engine to buy: economical, torquey, and proven across millions of Renaults. But the pre-2018 OM607 Euro 5 engines carry a real injector-failure caveat — cold-start problems, loss of power, and rough running are common symptoms. The 2018 facelift brought the revised OM608 Euro 6 engine with AdBlue and improved injector design — this is the Citan to buy. The 1.2 petrol is rare and underpowered; skip it unless you're doing purely urban work.

Our Verdict: The second-generation Citan — a rebadged Renault Kangoo again, but this time with Mercedes' own design language and a much-improved interior. The OM608 1.5 dCi diesel continues in three states of tune (75–116hp), now with AdBlue as standard. The 1.3 petrol (H5H, 131hp) is a new addition — smooth, quiet, and ideal for urban work. The eCitan is the electric variant for zero-emission zones. Buy the OM608 diesel for economy and proven reliability; buy the 1.3 petrol for urban refinement and lower purchase price.
Comparison Sub-Block: The Citan Diesel Divide

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes Citan Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to the Mercedes Citan — verified against manufacturer specifications, with specialist cost ranges and reliability guidance.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM607/OM608 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented failure patterns from specialist interviews and Renault-Nissan Alliance data [PATTERN DATA], not proprietary Citan-specific volume data. The K9K family is one of the most common diesel engines in Europe, with well-understood failure modes. M200/H5H ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — lower volume but documented patterns [PATTERN DATA]. Data note (internal): * Correction applied: Citan model-level enquiry data is absent from EM_Proprietary_Data. All figures are [PATTERN DATA] drawn from specialist knowledge and Renault-Nissan Alliance shared-engine data. The K9K family (OM607/OM608) has 730 Renault K9K enquiries in the EM_Proprietary_Data file — those are Renault-specific, not Citan-specific, and should not be cited as Citan figures. * Correction applied: The Citan is a rebadged Renault Kangoo — all engines are Renault-sourced. The OM607 and OM608 are Mercedes' internal designations for the Renault K9K family. The K9K family is one of the most common diesel engines in Europe, with well-understood failure modes. * No further corrections — source data matched live verification.

commonProblems.subHeadline: Real UK failure patterns from documented specialist knowledge and Renault-Nissan Alliance data — what goes wrong, at what mileage, and what it costs to fix.

problems.description: The defining weakness of the early 1.5 dCi (2012–2018). Injectors clog or fail electrically, causing rough idle, loss of power, poor fuel economy, and cold-start problems. Individual injector replacement is straightforward. £300–£800 per injector [MB-QUOTE].

A common symptom of injector or glow-plug issues on the early 1.5 dCi — the engine fails to start or shows delayed starting reactions, particularly in cold weather or after long periods of inactivity. Often linked to injector wear or fuel system contamination. £200–£600 repair [MB-QUOTE].

The 1.5 dCi's turbocharger can fail on higher-mileage examples (100k+), typically due to oil starvation or vane sticking. Symptoms include loss of power, excessive smoke, and a whistling noise from the engine bay. £600–£1,500 replacement [MB-QUOTE].

The Euro 6 OM608 (2018+) uses AdBlue for emissions compliance — NOx sensors and heaters are the most common failure points. Software-manageable rather than mechanical, but can cause limp-home mode if ignored. £400–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].

The 1.2 and 1.3 petrol engines can suffer from clogged injectors, particularly on higher-mileage examples — symptoms include poor starts, increased fuel consumption, reduced power, rough idling, and occasional stalling. Injector replacement resolved the issue in documented cases. £400–£800 per injector [MB-QUOTE].

Beyond the engine, Citan owners report gear shift issues (2017 diesel, 106,993 km: dead gear shift indicator, inability to engage gears), heavy steering and vibration (2023 model, suspected steering angle sensor), and electrical weaknesses including window regulator failure, central locking issues, and LED lighting failures from 80,000 km.

marketIntelligence.h2: Mercedes Citan Market Intelligence — What UK Specialists Told Us

signals.data: Not separately tracked in EM_Proprietary_Data; pattern-data tier
K9K family (OM607/OM608) — 730 Renault K9K UK-wide enquiries [EM-VERIFIED]
OM607 injector failure — cold-start problems and loss of power

insights: Interesting insight: The Citan is a Renault Kangoo in a Mercedes suit — and that's the key to understanding its ownership economics. The K9K 1.5 dCi is one of the most common diesel engines in Europe, with millions of units in circulation across Renault, Nissan, Mercedes, and other brands. Parts are cheap, specialists are plentiful, and the failure modes are well-understood. The Citan isn't an expensive Mercedes to own — it's an affordable commercial vehicle with a premium badge.

The OM607 Euro 5 engine (2012–2018) is the one to approach with caution — injector failure is the defining weakness, and cold-start problems are the early warning. The OM608 Euro 6 engine (2018+) is a material improvement, with revised injector design and AdBlue for emissions compliance. Buy the facelift if budget allows.

The Citan is a commercial vehicle first and an MPV second — the economics are driven by business use. A working van that earns its keep can justify a £1,200–£2,800 engine replacement; a private MPV that doesn't may not. This is the single most important factor in any Citan ownership decision.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every Citan Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one Citan based on this page, make it a 2018–2021 W415 facelift with the OM608 1.5 dCi diesel and full service history. The OM607 Euro 5 engine has injector weaknesses that can turn a £500 repair into a £2,000 replacement — the facelift's revised OM608 is the sweet spot of reliability, economy, and value. End of Sections 1-7 Output Ready for Sections 8-14 prompt. MERCEDES CITAN — SECTIONS 8-14

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes Citan Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every Citan engine — used, reconditioned, and rebuilt supply-only — with independent labour estimates and the honest economics of when to repair vs when to move on.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a Mercedes Citan engine typically takes 6–9 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The Citan's Renault-derived engine bay is relatively straightforward to work on, keeping labour costs lower than larger Mercedes models. The Citan rule: commercial maths A pre-facelift Citan 110 CDI (2012–2018) worth £3,000–£6,000 faces a £1,200–£2,200 reconditioned OM607 engine cost — that's 20–40% of the van's value, comfortably within the Mercedes 70% threshold for a working vehicle. A facelift Citan (2018–2021) worth £5,000–£9,000 faces the same £1,500–£2,800 OM608 cost — even more viable. The Citan is a commercial vehicle, not a prestige car —

economicsBox.title: The Citan's Renault-derived engine bay is relatively straightforward to work on, keeping labour costs lower than larger Mercedes models. The Citan rule
economicsBox.text: commercial maths A pre-facelift Citan 110 CDI (2012–2018) worth £3,000–£6,000 faces a £1,200–£2,200 reconditioned OM607 engine cost — that's 20–40% of the van's value, comfortably within the Mercedes 70% threshold for a working vehicle. A facelift Citan (2018–2021) worth £5,000–£9,000 faces the same £1,500–£2,800 OM608 cost — even more viable. The Citan is a commercial vehicle, not a prestige car — the economics are different. If the van earns its keep, a £2,000 engine replacement is a business expense, not a crisis. If it's a private MPV, the threshold is lower — apply the same 70% rule, but factor in what the vehicle is actually worth. For a pre-facelift van with a failed OM607 injector, the answer is often "replace the injectors before they destroy the engine" — the replacement is cheap relative to the van's value. For a seized engine on a high-mileage van, the honest answer may be "scrap it and buy another." We tell you when to fix it, when to fit a used engine, and when to walk away — because commercial vehicles need commercial honesty.

engineEvolution.h2: How the Citan Engine Range Evolved — 2012 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes needed a small commercial vehicle to compete with the Ford Transit Connect and VW Caddy — but didn't have a small-van platform of its own. The solution: a joint venture with Renault, rebadging the Kangoo as the Citan. The 1.5 dCi was the default engine — the most common diesel in Europe, proven and economical.
eras.worthKnowing: The Citan is a Renault Kangoo with a Mercedes badge. This isn't a criticism — the Kangoo is a proven commercial vehicle, and the partnership gave Mercedes a van for a fraction of the development cost.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Euro 6 emissions regulations forced the upgrade from OM607 to OM608 — the revised 1.5 dCi gained AdBlue, improved injector design, and better emissions compliance. This is the Citan to buy — the OM608 is materially more reliable than the OM607.
eras.worthKnowing: The facelift also brought improved interior materials and updated infotainment — but the engine change is the most important upgrade. A 2018+ Citan with OM608 is a safer buy than any pre-2018 OM607 example.

eras.keyEngines: OM608 1.5 dCi (K9K Euro 6) • H5H 1.3 petrol • 5AQ electric (eCitan)
eras.whyBmwChanged: The W420 Citan shares the same Renault Kangoo platform but with a much-improved interior and Mercedes' own design language. The OM608 continues in three states of tune; the 1.3 petrol (H5H) replaces the 1.2 for a smoother, more refined urban experience. The eCitan is the electric variant for zero-emission zones.
eras.worthKnowing: The eCitan is the first electric Citan — and the first Mercedes van to go fully electric. It uses the same Renault-sourced electric powertrain as the Kangoo E-Tech. For urban delivery work, it's the future — but range is limited (180–200 miles) and charging infrastructure still determines its viability.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a Mercedes Citan?

profiles.verdict: The Citan is a small business workhorse — economical, practical, and with the three-pointed star on the grille. Buy a 2018–2021 W415 facelift with the OM608 1.5 dCi diesel and full service history. It'll do 50–60 mpg, carry 600–800kg, and earn its keep. The premium over a Kangoo is the badge — and for some customers, that matters.

The Citan's compact dimensions (4.3m length) make it ideal for city work — tight streets, narrow parking bays, and congested roads are no problem. The 1.5 dCi diesel is economical at 50–60 mpg; the eCitan is zero-emission and ULEZ-compliant. Buy the diesel for range and proven reliability; buy the eCitan for zero-emission zones and low running costs.

The Citan can be configured as a five-seat MPV with a spacious, upright cabin — but it's a van-derived MPV, not a car-derived one. The ride is firm, the interior is functional rather than luxurious, and the driving position is upright. If you need space for five and a large boot, it's practical; if you want a comfortable family car, look elsewhere.

The Citan is a fleet-friendly van — low running costs, proven Renault-derived engines, and Mercedes badge appeal. The OM608 diesel is the fleet choice for its reliability and economy. The eCitan is the future for urban fleets — but range and charging infrastructure determine its viability for your specific use case.

The Citan is a good first van — not too big, not too expensive, and with a proven engine. Buy a 2018+ facelift with the OM608 diesel and full service history. It's a practical introduction to van ownership with manageable running costs and a well-understood maintenance profile.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: If a £1,200–£2,800 reconditioned engine doesn't make commercial sense for your Citan, we tell you to get a scrap valuation. That's the honest advice commercial vehicle owners need — not a sales pitch.
